metadata.seed: My partner was seeing someone else on the side

patient.query: My partner was seeing someone else on the side

automatic_thoughts: I'm not enough
They don't love me
I can't trust anyone

llm_response.response:
It sounds like you’re feeling deeply hurt and betrayed by what happened.  
It’s understandable to question your worth and feel upset in this situation.  
You’re not alone in experiencing these emotions.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess so. It just feels really heavy, you know? Like, one minute everything seemed fine, and then it all just fell apart. I can't shake this feeling that maybe I wasn't enough for them.

automatic_thoughts: maybe I wasn't enough for them

llm_response.response: It sounds like this heaviness is tied to a sense that you might not have met someone’s expectations. What evidence do you have that you were or weren’t enough for them? How might you view the situation if you considered other possible explanations or perspectives?

patient.query: I don’t know… I just keep thinking about little things, like how they would get frustrated or pull away when I didn’t do things their way. It’s hard to see it any other way right now. I just feel—maybe it's silly—but like I should've done more. Maybe if I had, things would be different.

automatic_thoughts: I should've done more
maybe if I had, things would be different

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy sense of guilt over how things might have turned out if you had acted differently. What evidence do you have that doing more would have changed the outcome? Sometimes looking at the situation from a different angle can help us see that we did what we could with the information we had at the time.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I keep wondering if I missed signs or if I was too caught up in my own stuff to really see what they needed. It’s like I replay those moments in my mind, thinking about how I could’ve handled things better. But then, when I think about it, I didn't really know how they felt. It’s frustrating. I feel stuck, like I keep going in circles.

automatic_thoughts: I missed signs
I was too caught up in my own stuff
I could've handled things better
I didn't really know how they felt
behaviors: replay those moments in my mind

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of what‑ifs. What evidence do you have that you missed signs, and how might you view those moments from a different angle? How could you reframe the idea that you didn’t know how they felt, perhaps by considering what you do know about their reactions?

patient.query: I guess it just feels easier to blame myself. But, yeah, maybe I should think about how they didn’t really communicate things either. There were times when they would just shut down instead of talking it out. It makes me wonder if it was really all on me. It’s just hard to let go of that feeling. I feel like I should’ve been able to read them better.

automatic_thoughts: I should blame myself
it's all on me
I should've read them better

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of responsibility for how things turned out. What evidence do you have that the communication breakdown was mainly your fault? If you were talking to a close friend in the same situation, what would you tell them?

patient.query: Yeah, I do feel that way. I keep thinking about things I said or didn’t say, like I could’ve changed the outcome. But if I were talking to a friend, I guess I’d probably tell them that it takes two people to make a relationship work, and it’s not all on them. Still, it’s hard to apply that to myself. I just feel so lost right now, like I don’t even know how to make sense of it all.

automatic_thoughts: I could’ve changed the outcome
It takes two people to make a relationship work
It's not all on me
I don't even know how to make sense of it all

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response:
It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of regret and feeling lost right now.  
When you think about the idea that it takes two people to make a relationship work, what does that feel like for you?  
What might be one small thing you could do to start making sense of these feelings?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: It takes two people to make a relationship work
retrieved_concepts_used.label: automatic_thoughts

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: I don’t even know how to make sense of it all
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query:
Yeah, it does feel heavy. The idea that it takes two just makes me feel... I don’t know, almost like I’m torn. Part of me wants to believe it, but another part still thinks I should've done more. Like, I keep thinking if I had just tried harder, maybe we wouldn’t have ended up here.

As for doing something small… maybe I could write it all down? Just to get it out of my head. But even that feels overwhelming sometimes, like

triggers: the idea that it takes two
automatic_thoughts: I should've done more
if I had just tried harder, maybe we wouldn’t have ended up here

neutral: Working through the pain of grief (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy mix of guilt and grief, and that’s completely understandable. Feeling torn between wanting to believe the idea that it takes two and thinking you could have done more is a common struggle in this kind of loss. Writing everything down can be a gentle way to give those thoughts a space to breathe, even if it feels overwhelming at first.

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel like a lot sometimes. I mean, in some ways, I think it could help to get it all out, but I also worry about what I’d actually write. It’s like, what if I dig deeper and discover more things that hurt? I don’t know if I’m ready for that. I just feel… kind of raw, you know?

automatic_thoughts: it could help to get it all out
what if I discover more things that hurt?
I don’t know if I’m ready for that

neutral: Working through the pain of grief (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling both hopeful about getting things out and cautious about what you might uncover. What do you think would happen if you did decide to write it all down? How might that experience feel for you, and what could you do to support yourself through it?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess it could be a bit of both. Writing it down might help me understand what I’m really feeling, but it also scares me to think about facing those feelings head-on. I worry it might just make me feel even worse. I mean, supporting myself… I don’t even know what that looks like right now. I feel so fragile, like I might break if I push too hard. Maybe I could take it slow, just write a little bit each time?

automatic_thoughts: it might just make me feel even worse
I don’t even know what that looks like
I might break if I push too hard
behaviors: write a little bit each time

llm_response.response:
I hear how uneasy you feel about putting your emotions on paper—it's understandable to worry that it could amplify the distress.  
What evidence do you have that writing will make you feel worse, and have there been moments when writing helped you feel a bit clearer or calmer?  
If you tried a very small, safe amount—just a sentence or two—what might that look like for you, and how could you gauge whether it feels helpful or not?

patient.query:
I guess I don’t really have solid evidence that writing will make me feel worse; it’s more of a fear, you know? I’ve tried journaling before, and sometimes it helped me feel a little lighter, but it also brought up some really tough stuff I wasn't ready to deal with. 

If I tried just a sentence or two, maybe I could start by writing something small about how the breakup made me feel, like “I feel sad and confused.” I could see if just expressing

automatic_thoughts: writing will make me feel worse
I feel sad and confused
behaviors: consider writing a sentence or two

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re weighing the possibility that writing could stir up painful feelings, but you also see a chance to try something small. What kinds of thoughts or memories come up when you think about writing, and how do you know they might be “too much” for you right now? If you start with just a sentence or two, how might that feel different from a longer entry, and what could you do if something uncomfortable surfaces?
